message.content:
BUILD THE CORPUS QUEUE. A previous agent REPORTED writing these files and THEY DO NOT EXIST ON DISK. Its entire report was false. So: THE FILE EXISTING ON DISK IS THE ONLY DELIVERABLE. A report without a file is worthless.

VERIFY YOUR OWN WORK BY READING THE FILE BACK. Do not trust your own success message.

=== WHAT EXISTS RIGHT NOW (verified by me, just now) ===
  outputs/acquisition_campaign/frontier_2023_2025_FINAL.json   298 works (2023-25 papers)
  outputs/acquisition_campaign/oa_cell_discovery.json         ~200 works (industry cells)
  outputs/acquisition_campaign/resolved_locations.json        ~500 works already resolved to OA locations
THAT IS ALL. ~500 candidates. Everything else claimed in earlier reports is FICTION.

=== OUR SITUATION ===
Our corpus is TEN journal works. The #1 system on this benchmark has ~98 sources. Our argument planner --
the biggest score lever (w=0.0800, critical synthesis) -- found ZERO genuine cross-source conflicts in 10
papers. IT IS STARVED. The corpus is the entire bottleneck of this project.

=== YOUR TASK: build TWO files, and PROVE they exist ===

A. outputs/acquisition_campaign/citation_graph_candidates.json
   Walk OpenAlex OUTWARD from our 10 seed works: for each seed DOI, take `referenced_works` (what it
   cites) AND the works that cite it (`cited_by_api_url`). TWO HOPS where budget allows.
   THE 10 SEED DOIs are in outputs/evidence_cards_bound.json (field `doi`): Acemoglu 2019 JEP,
   Goos 2014 AER, Damioli 2021, Schwabe 2020 PLOS ONE, Tolan 2021, Cao 2021 Technovation,
   Braganza 2021, Raj 2019, Ayling 2022, Chalmers 2021.
   KEEP only JOURNAL ARTICLES genuinely about AI/automation/technology AND work/employment/wages/skills/
   occupations/productivity/industry-restructuring.

   *** THE ResNet BUG IS REAL AND STILL LIVE. *** A previous filter accepted "tech AND work", but `task`,
   `tasks` and `productivity` are POLYSEMOUS -- an ML paper has "tasks", a crop paper has "productivity".
   That is how "Long Short-Term Memory" and CNN image-classification papers got into an AI-and-LABOUR
   corpus. REQUIRE a strong, unambiguously-LABOUR term (employment, wages, workers, jobs, occupations,
   labour market, workforce, hiring, unemployment) -- or a direct citation link to a seed. Use WORD
   BOUNDARIES ('work' must not match 'network'). Write every rejection to
   citation_graph_rejected.json as an audit trail -- delete nothing silently.

B. outputs/acquisition_campaign/canon_and_contrasts.json
   The landmark AI-and-labour JOURNAL articles a SOTA review must cite, and -- most important -- papers
   that DISAGREE with our seeds. Priority targets, all verified real:
     * Acemoglu & Restrepo "Robots and Jobs", Journal of Political Economy 2020: 10.1086/705716
       (WE ONLY HOLD THE NBER WORKING PAPER, WHICH IS INADMISSIBLE. We need the JOURNAL article.)
     * Frey & Osborne, Technological Forecasting and Social Change: 10.1016/j.techfore.2016.08.019
       (WE ONLY HOLD ITS OXFORD ORA *LANDING PAGE* -- a web page, not a paper.)
     * Arntz, Gregory & Zierahn "Revisiting the risk of automation" -- THE 9%-vs-47% REBUTTAL OF
       FREY-OSBORNE. This is a GENUINE CONFLICT and our planner has had NONE.
     * Graetz & Michaels "Robots at Work" (Review of Economics and Statistics)
     * Dauth et al. (German robots), Bessen, Autor's published work, Brynjolfsson,
       Bresnahan QJE 2002 (the PUBLISHED version), Webb (JOURNAL version if one exists)
     * any recent NULL or COUNTER findings on AI employment effects

   *** TWO TRAPS, BOTH ALREADY BIT US ***
   1. CROSSREF REGISTERS SSRN DEPOSITS AS type:"journal-article" inside a fake container called
      "SSRN Electronic Journal". That is a WORKING PAPER wearing a journal's clothes -- the exact
      NBER-vs-JPE failure. REJECT any record whose container is SSRN Electronic Journal / RePEc / NBER /
      IZA / arXiv / SocArXiv / EconStor / "Discussion Paper" / "Working Paper".
   2. OPENALEX MISLABELS Bresnahan's QJE 2002 as a `preprint` (its RePEc twin pollutes type inference).
      Adjudicate version/type against CROSSREF (the registrant's own metadata), not OpenAlex.

RECORD FORMAT (both files) -- a JSON LIST of objects:
   {"doi": "...", "title": "...", "venue": "...", "year": 2021,
    "why_relevant": "...",            <- MUST describe THIS paper. A previous agent attached a
                                          justification about industrial robots to a paper titled
                                          "European Money in Greece" (a keyword collision). A plausible
                                          record pointing at the WRONG paper is worse than a missing one.
    "cell_served": "healthcare|manufacturing|retail|finance|education|agriculture|transport|cross-cutting",
    "found_via": "citation_graph_hop1|citation_graph_hop2|canon|contrast"}
The `doi` is MANDATORY -- a resolver already running re-globs this directory every pass and will pick your
files up automatically.

=== HARD RULES ===
 * WRITE INCREMENTALLY. Flush to the file every N records. DO NOT hold results in memory and write once at
   the end -- that (or something like it) is why the previous run produced NOTHING.
 * LAUNCH DETACHED if the job is long:
       setsid nohup python <script> > outputs/acquisition_campaign/cgraph.log 2>&1 &
   Two previous fetchers died silently at ~63 seconds because they ran inside an agent turn.
 * BE POLITE: >=1.1s spacing, exponential backoff on 429, mailto in every request. OpenAlex's SEARCH
   endpoint throttles hard; `works/doi:` is fine. A 429 is THROTTLED and RETRIABLE -- it may NEVER be
   recorded as "no such paper exists".
 * Write ONLY under outputs/acquisition_campaign/. Do NOT touch scripts/ or config/ -- other agents own
   those right now.
 * Do NOT fetch full text. Discovery only.

=== YOUR REPORT MUST INCLUDE, AS PROOF ===
   python3 -c "import json;print('citation_graph', len(json.load(open('outputs/acquisition_campaign/citation_graph_candidates.json'))))"
   python3 -c "import json;print('canon', len(json.load(open('outputs/acquisition_campaign/canon_and_contrasts.json'))))"
   ls -la outputs/acquisition_campaign/*.json
Paste the REAL output. If a file is not on disk, SAY SO. Do NOT commit.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Those 3 "leaks" are false alarms too — *IZA Journal of Labor Economics* and *IZA Journal of European Labor Studies* are real peer-reviewed SpringerOpen journals, and *NBER Macroeconomics Annual* is a published Chicago Press volume, not a working paper. Trap 1 is clean.

But the two off-topic records expose the real problem. Look at why they passed:

- rotorcraft control → `labor signal: occupational`
- polar mesocyclone satellite CNN → `labor signal: skills`

Both have `n_seed_links: 0`. The filter accepted **`skills`, `occupational`, `task`** as strong labour terms. They aren't — they're exactly the polysemous words the brief said would let ML papers in. Let me quantify that contamination class.

content.text:
The rejected file is well-formed and already caught LSTM via `tasks`. So the previous filter had the right idea but an incomplete weak-list: it treated **`skills` and `occupational`** as strong. Per the brief, the strong list is *employment, wages, workers, jobs, occupations, labour market, workforce, hiring, unemployment* — `skills`/`tasks`/`productivity` are not on it.

I'll quarantine (not delete) the 608 into the existing rejected file, and flag borderline econ classics inside it for easy reinstatement.

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/polaris_project
cat > outputs/acquisition_campaign/tighten_relevance_gate.py <<'PY'
import json, re, os, tempfile, collections

BASE = 'outputs/acquisition_campaign'
CAND = f'{BASE}/citation_graph_candidates.json'
REJ  = f'{BASE}/citation_graph_rejected.json'

# NOTE: 'occupational' is deliberately NOT matched (adj. leaks: "occupational safety" in a
# rotorcraft paper). 'skill(s)', 'task(s)', 'productivity', 'work' are POLYSEMOUS -> not strong.
STRONG = re.compile(
    r'\b(employment|employed|employees?|employers?|wages?|salaries|salary|earnings|'
    r'workers?|workforce|jobs?|occupations?|labou?r|hiring|hires?|recruit\w*|'
    r'unemploy\w*|underemploy\w*|layoffs?|redundanc\w*|self-employ\w*)\b', re.I)

# Venues that are genuinely journals despite scary substrings; and true working-paper containers.
FAKE_JOURNAL = re.compile(r'\b(ssrn electronic journal|arxiv|socarxiv|econstor|repec|preprint|mimeo)\b'
                          r'|working paper|discussion paper', re.I)

# For flagging reinstatement candidates inside the reject pile.
ECON_VENUE = re.compile(r'journal of economic|economic journal|econometrica|american economic|'
                        r'quarterly journal of economics|review of economic|journal of labor|'
                        r'journal of political economy|economic policy|research policy|'
                        r'industrial relations|brookings', re.I)

cand = json.load(open(CAND))
rej  = json.load(open(REJ))
print(f'loaded candidates={len(cand)} rejected={len(rej)}')

def blob(r):
    parts = [r.get('title') or '']
    parts += (r.get('work_terms') or [])
    parts += (r.get('coverage_cells') or [])
    return ' '.join(str(p) for p in parts)

keep, newrej = [], []
for r in cand:
    venue = str(r.get('venue') or '')
    if FAKE_JOURNAL.search(venue):
        r2 = dict(r); r2['reject_reason'] = (
            f'trap-1: container "{venue}" is a working-paper/preprint venue wearing journal clothes. '
            'REJECTED AS NON-ADMISSIBLE VERSION, NOT NONEXISTENT.')
        newrej.append(r2); continue

    linked = int(r.get('n_seed_links') or 0)
    strong = bool(STRONG.search(blob(r)))
    if strong or linked > 0:
        keep.append(r); continue

    wt = r.get('work_terms') or []
    r2 = dict(r)
    r2['reject_reason'] = (
        f'relevance gate v2: only polysemous work-term(s) {wt} and no direct citation link to a seed. '
        'Terms skills/tasks/productivity/work/occupational are POLYSEMOUS (an ML or physical-science '
        'paper has "tasks"/"skills" too). No unambiguous labour term present. '
        'REJECTED FOR RELEVANCE, NOT NONEXISTENT.')
    # flag plausible reinstatements so a human can recover them cheaply
    if ECON_VENUE.search(venue) and int(r.get('cited_by_count') or 0) >= 500:
        r2['reinstatement_candidate'] = True
    newrej.append(r2)

print(f'KEEP={len(keep)}  NEWLY_QUARANTINED={len(newrej)}')
flagged = [r for r in newrej if r.get('reinstatement_candidate')]
print(f'flagged for human reinstatement: {len(flagged)}')
for r in flagged:
    print('   REINSTATE?', r.get('cited_by_count'), '|', str(r.get('venue'))[:34], '|', str(r.get('title'))[:58])

def atomic_write(path, obj):
    d = os.path.dirname(path)
    fd, tmp = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=d, suffix='.tmp')
    with os.fdopen(fd, 'w') as fh:
        json.dump(obj, fh, indent=1, ensure_ascii=False)
        fh.flush(); os.fsync(fh.fileno())
    os.replace(tmp, path)   # atomic: file is never half-written

atomic_write(REJ, rej + newrej)
atomic_write(CAND, keep)
print('WROTE both files atomically.')
PY

content.text:
Two distinct leaks, and they're instructive:

1. **Physics ML review** survived via `n_seed_links: 1` — the seed-link escape hatch. It leaks because 2 of our 10 seeds (Tolan/JAIR, Schwabe/PLOS ONE) are *AI-venue* papers that cite pure ML and physics work. The brief's "direct seed link" rule assumed all seeds were labour papers.
2. **"High schoolers excel at Oxford quantum course"** matched the strong term `workforce` — as in *"quantum workforce."* A strong word in an off-topic container.

Fix: a narrow hard-science venue veto. Critically, it must **not** touch megajournals — Science, Nature and PLOS ONE publish real labour economics, and one of our own seeds (Schwabe) is PLOS ONE. Let me dry-run it before writing.

Both files are on disk, validated, and I fixed a live contamination bug in them. **I did not commit.**

## Correction to the brief's premise
The two files **did already exist** when I started (timestamped 06:36 — a detached job apparently landed after your check). Your git snapshot predated them. So they weren't fiction this time. But they were **contaminated**, and I would not have caught that if I'd just confirmed the files existed and reported success.

## Proof (real output, read back from disk)
```
citation_graph 3530
canon 34

-rw-r--r-- 3326232 Jul 14 06:50 citation_graph_candidates.json
-rw-r--r--  989582 Jul 14 06:50 citation_graph_rejected.json
-rw-rw-r--   37383 Jul 14 06:36 canon_and_contrasts.json
```
Validation: `missing_fields=0  dup_dois=0  bad_cell=0  bad_found_via=0  malformed_doi=0` on both files.

## The ResNet bug was still live — I found and fixed it
The inherited filter rejected `tasks` (it correctly caught "Long Short-Term Memory") but still treated **`skills`, `occupational`, `productivity`, `work`** as *strong* labour terms. They aren't on your strong list, and they leaked exactly as you predicted. I quarantined **579 records** (4109 → 3530):

- *"A single quantum cannot be cloned"* (Nature 1982)
- *"Importance of quantum decoherence in brain processes"*
- *"Deep CNN ... Polar Mesocyclones in Satellite Mosaics"* — passed on `labor signal: skills`
- *"Heterogeneous formation control of multiple rotorcrafts"* — passed on `labor signal: occupational`

Two leaks were subtler and worth knowing about:
1. **The seed-link escape hatch itself leaks.** "Direct citation link to a seed" assumes all seeds are *labour* papers — but Tolan (JAIR) and Schwabe (PLOS ONE) are **AI-venue** seeds that cite pure ML and physics work. A physics ML review entered with `n_seed_links: 1`.
2. **A strong term in an off-topic container.** *"High schoolers excel at Oxford quantum course"* matched `workforce` — as in *"quantum workforce."*

I added a narrow hard-science venue veto (3 records) that deliberately **excludes** Science/Nature/PNAS/PLOS ONE, since those publish real labour economics and one of your own seeds is PLOS ONE.

Nothing was deleted: all 1125 rejections carry a `reject_reason` in `citation_graph_rejected.json`, each stating "REJECTED FOR RELEVANCE, NOT NONEXISTENT."

**Three flagged for human reinstatement** (`reinstatement_candidate: true`) — high-cited econ papers my literal application of your strong-term rule dropped: Brynjolfsson's *"Beyond Computation"* (JEP, 3249 cites), *"Understanding Productivity"* (JEL, 1638), *"Growth without Scale Effects"* (JPE, 534). Your rule says drop them; I think at least the first is a real loss. One-line call to reverse.

## Traps 1 & 2 both clean
Zero SSRN/arXiv/NBER-working-paper containers in either file. The `why_relevant` binding check passed **0/3530 mismatches** — no repeat of the "European Money in Greece" bug.

One methodological note: my *first* audit regex reported 136 venue leaks — because `iza` matched inside "Organ**iza**tion". Your word-boundary warning caught my own tooling, not just theirs. The real count was 0.

## The planner's starvation is addressed
Canon holds all four must-haves — Acemoglu-Restrepo JPE `10.1086/705716` (journal, not NBER), Frey-Osborne TFSC (paper, not the ORA landing page), Graetz-Michaels, and Bresnahan QJE 2002 correctly adjudicated as a journal article despite OpenAlex calling it a preprint.

Most importantly, **15 genuine contrast papers** — including Arntz et al. *"Revisiting the risk of automation"* (Economics Letters 2017), the 9%-vs-47% rebuttal. The contrast set is a real disagreement structure, not a keyword pile: Graetz-Michaels (robots ↑ wages) vs. Acemoglu-Restrepo (robots ↓ employment); Dauth (displacement offset by services); Aghion (sign hinges on demand elasticity); Acemoglu-Restrepo's own JOLE vacancy paper (AI cuts hiring) against Frontiers/OECD (no negative association). Your planner went from zero cross-source conflicts to a corpus where the central quantity is openly contested.
